And, um, is the regional Council where, um,
the administrating authority
for the Mantu Wgnu Civil Defense Emergency Management Group.
Our submission focuses on strengthening the implementation
to ensure the system works in practice, not just in stature.
We focus on clarity, integration
and resourcing in the current environment
with proposed rates keeping
and concurrent reforms
to resource management and local government.
This creates a real implementation risk.
Councils may be permitted to increase rates
after a disaster,
but not enabled
to invest adequately in the preparedness beforehand.
We know disaster preparedness delivers a strong return.
The RIS is estimated this at 4.1, uh, sorry,
four to one, but there is still an upfront cost
in our region.
This is not theoretical.
We are the only region in New Zealand without a rain radar,
which significantly compromises our ability to prepare
for short notice extreme weather events.
We strongly support strengthening Iwi
and Hapu participation.
The bill recognizes their value,
but does not provide funding to support this
participation in our region.
Marai provide critical welfare support and emergencies.
They should not be left carrying the financial burden
in alignment with other major reforms.
The emergency management does not operate in isolation.
Hazard responsibilities currently span the CDM Act, the RMA
and the Local Government Act.
These are all under reform as drafted.
There are risk of inconsistent terminology,
overlapping hazard planning functions
and misalignment between regional plans, spatial plans,
and the National Emergency management plan.
These inconsistencies create uncertainty for implementation.
Flood protection infrastructure providers should be added
to Schedule three as essential infrastructure
and water service providers that provide only a minor aspect
of water services should be excluded
disproportionately affected.
Communities must be defined to ensure consistent
identification of these communities
and that addressing these their concerns sits at the
appropriate level of government.
Meaningful consultation,
consultation timeframe should be specified
at least 20 working days for national strategies, plans
and regulations, arrangements for
how offers of assistance will be managed.
And arra arrangements
for animal welfare should be both set nationally.
In closing, horizon supports the direction of reform.
A region knows firsthand the importance of a strong, clear
and coordinated emergency management system.
Legislation alone will not strengthen the system.
Implementation depends on resourcing clarity
and alignment with targeted amendments, particularly
around funding, iwi participation
and legislative integration.
This bill could deliver a resilient, equitable
and nationally consistent emergency management system.

three technical aspects that particularly impact Auckland.
So the bill reflects Auckland's own improvement pathway
since the North island severe weather events of 2023
with tangible progress, um, um, in building our capability,
our capacity, and our community connection.
And that's captured in our group plan
and our subsequent reporting.
Our submission states
that Auckland Council broadly supports the bill
and the need for a fully funded
national improvement program.
Our submission recommends several key refinements based on
our lived experience in Auckland,
and namely, clearer recognition of the crucial role
of individuals, whanau, iwi, and communities.
A stronger positioning of iwi Maori
as enduring partners within the emergency san uh,
management system, enabling recovery to grow
to the same level of maturity as response
and addressing compliance costs
and that risk of cost shifting to local government.
The submission also recommends some technical amendments in
support of our submission points,
and I'll just elaborate on three of these.
Um, first of all, around the change definition of emergency,
the council supports the updated definition of emergency,
which now focuses on whether an event requires a significant
coordinated response.
We believe this wording will make it easier
for decision makers to determine when the threshold
for declaring a state of emergency has been met.
Um, we reflect that following the 2023 weather events,
there has been strong public scrutiny around the timing
of declarations, particularly in Auckland.
There is now a greater understanding
that a declaration is not only an operational tool,
it also plays an important role in signaling the seriousness
of an event, providing reassurance,
and also helping the public respond appropriately.
Given this shift in expectations,
Auckland Council would welcome updated national guidance.
Consistent guidance would help ensure declarations are
applied uniformly across the sector.
And it would also be helpful for that guidance to clarify
how far declarations should be used
as a public information tool while still recognizing the
extraordinary powers that accompany a state of emergency.
My second point relates to, um, who is authorized
to declare a state of emergency,
and this is a particularly Auckland, um, issue.
So we would like to highlight that the provisions relating
to who is authorized to declare a state of emergency, um,
are unclear when applied to Auckland.
And this relates to clauses 49
and 50, which set out two different pathways.
Clause 49 allows authorized persons appointed
by the Emergency Management Committee to declare
for whole areas multiple districts or multiple wards.
While clause 50 gives mayors
of territorial authorities the power to declare
for individual districts or wards within their districts.
So Auckland, um, as you may be aware,
does not have multiple districts.
It's a unitary authority with a single district.
As a result, it's unclear how Clause 50 is intended
to operate here in Auckland.
Specifically, we, um, request clarity on whether the Mayor
of Auckland is empowered by Clause 50 part two
for declarations affecting the whole Auckland District,
or whether the mayor must instead be an appointed,
be appointed as an authorized person under clause 49.
If clause 50 part two is intended to apply.
It would also be helpful to clarify whether the hierarchy
exists between whether a hierarchy exists between Clause 49
and clause 50 in determining
who should make a declaration for the Auckland area.
And additionally, based on current drafting, it appears
that in a unitary, unitary authority context,
where there's only one district
that only the mayor is authorized to declare
for a single ward, as there is no provision
for an authorized person to do so in clause 49.
Um, that also, um, flows on, we believe into our, um,
the authorities of our controllers,
and we've set that out in recommendation three.
So I won't repeat it here.

Or does the bill intend

in all cases, that would be a significant departure from
